```{r}
  labs(x = NULL, y = NULL, fill = NULL,
       title = "Variable Identification Across Three Methods") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom",
        panel.grid = element_blank()) +
  coord_cartesian(clip = "off")
```

## Figure: BMA PIP vs. WALS |t-statistic|

```{r}
#| label: fig-pip-vs-t
#| code-summary: "Scatter plot: BMA PIP vs WALS |t-stat|, with LASSO selection as shape"
#| fig-cap: "BMA PIP vs WALS |t-statistic|. Point shape indicates LASSO selection. Variables in the upper-right quadrant are robust by both BMA and WALS."
#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-height: 6

grand_table_plot <- grand_table |>
  mutate(
    lasso_shape = ifelse(lasso_selected, "LASSO: Selected", "LASSO: Not selected"),
    true_status = ifelse(true_nonzero, "True predictor", "Noise")
  )

ggplot(grand_table_plot, aes(x = abs(wals_t), y = bma_pip)) +
  # Quadrant shading
  annotate("rect", xmin = 2, xmax = Inf, ymin = 0.80, ymax = 1,
           fill = "#e8f5e9", alpha = 0.5) +
  annotate("text", x = 5, y = 0.90, label = "Robust by\nboth methods",
           size = 3, color = "gray40") +
  # Threshold lines
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0.80, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray40") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 2, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray40") +
  # Points
  geom_point(aes(color = true_status, shape = lasso_shape), size = 4) +
  # Labels
  geom_text_repel(aes(label = variable), size = 3, max.overlaps = 20) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("True predictor" = "#2c7bb6", "Noise" = "#d7191c")) +
  scale_shape_manual(values = c("LASSO: Selected" = 17, "LASSO: Not selected" = 4)) +
  labs(x = "WALS |t-statistic|",
       y = "BMA Posterior Inclusion Probability",
       color = "True status", shape = "LASSO result",
       title = "BMA PIP vs. WALS |t-statistic|",
       subtitle = "Upper-right = robust by both; triangles = LASSO-selected") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

## Figure: Coefficient Comparison

```{r}
#| label: fig-coef-comparison
#| code-summary: "Compare true coefficients with BMA, Post-LASSO, and WALS estimates"
#| fig-cap: "Coefficient estimates from the three methods compared to the true values. Points close to the dashed 45-degree line indicate accurate recovery."
#| fig-width: 9
#| fig-height: 6

# Get Post-LASSO coefficients for all variables (0 if not selected)
post_lasso_all <- tibble(
  variable = names(true_beta_lookup),
  post_lasso = 0
)
if (nrow(post_lasso_summary) > 0) {
  post_lasso_all <- post_lasso_all |>
    left_join(post_lasso_summary |> select(variable, post_lasso_coef), by = "variable") |>
    mutate(post_lasso = coalesce(post_lasso_coef, 0)) |>
    select(variable, post_lasso)
}

# Combine all coefficient estimates
coef_compare <- grand_table |>
  select(variable, true_beta, bma_postmean, wals_estimate) |>
  left_join(post_lasso_all, by = "variable") |>
  pivot_longer(c(bma_postmean, post_lasso, wals_estimate),
               names_to = "method", values_to = "estimate") |>
  mutate(
    method = recode(method,
                    bma_postmean = "BMA Post. Mean",
                    post_lasso = "Post-LASSO",
                    wals_estimate = "WALS"),
    method = factor(method, levels = c("BMA Post. Mean", "Post-LASSO", "WALS"))
  )

ggplot(coef_compare, aes(x = true_beta, y = estimate, color = method, shape = method)) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray50") +
  geom_point(size = 3, alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_text_repel(aes(label = variable), size = 2.5, max.overlaps = 20,
                  show.legend = FALSE) +
  facet_wrap(~method, nrow = 1) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("BMA Post. Mean" = "#2c7bb6",
                                 "Post-LASSO" = "#fdae61",
                                 "WALS" = "#d7191c")) +
  labs(x = "True Coefficient",
       y = "Estimated Coefficient",
       color = "Method", shape = "Method",
       title = "Coefficient Recovery: Estimated vs. True",
       subtitle = "Points on the dashed line = perfect recovery") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")
```

## Figure: Agreement Summary

```{r}
#| label: fig-agreement
#| code-summary: "Bar chart: how many methods identified each variable"
#| fig-cap: "Number of methods (out of 3) that identified each variable as robust. Green bars are true predictors; red bars are noise variables."
#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-height: 5

grand_table |>
  mutate(
    bar_fill = ifelse(true_nonzero, "True predictor", "Noise")
  ) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(variable, n_methods), y = n_methods, fill = bar_fill)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.6) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 3, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray40") +
  annotate("text", x = 0.5, y = 3.1, label = "Triple-robust", hjust = 0, size = 3) +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("True predictor" = "#2c7bb6", "Noise" = "#d7191c")) +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = 0:3) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Number of methods identifying variable as robust",
       fill = "True status",
       title = "Agreement Across Three Methods",
       subtitle = "How many methods agree that each variable is robust?") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

## Discussion

```{r}
#| label: discussion-stats
#| code-summary: "Compute summary statistics for discussion"

# Count true/false positives and negatives for each method
results_by_method <- tibble(
  method = c("BMA", "LASSO", "WALS"),
  true_pos = c(
    sum(grand_table$bma_robust & grand_table$true_nonzero),
    sum(grand_table$lasso_selected & grand_table$true_nonzero),
    sum(grand_table$wals_robust & grand_table$true_nonzero)
  ),
  false_pos = c(
    sum(grand_table$bma_robust & !grand_table$true_nonzero),
    sum(grand_table$lasso_selected & !grand_table$true_nonzero),
    sum(grand_table$wals_robust & !grand_table$true_nonzero)
  ),
  false_neg = c(
    sum(!grand_table$bma_robust & grand_table$true_nonzero),
    sum(!grand_table$lasso_selected & grand_table$true_nonzero),
    sum(!grand_table$wals_robust & grand_table$true_nonzero)
  ),
  true_neg = c(
    sum(!grand_table$bma_robust & !grand_table$true_nonzero),
    sum(!grand_table$lasso_selected & !grand_table$true_nonzero),
    sum(!grand_table$wals_robust & !grand_table$true_nonzero)
  )
)

# Triple-robust variables
triple_robust_vars <- grand_table |>
  filter(triple_robust) |>
  pull(variable)

# Variables missed by all methods
all_missed <- grand_table |>
  filter(n_methods == 0 & true_nonzero) |>
  pull(variable)
```

**Which variables are "triple-robust"?** The variables identified by all three methods---`r paste(triple_robust_vars, collapse = ", ")`---are the ones we can be most confident about. When BMA, LASSO, and WALS all agree, the evidence is very strong.

**Were any noise variables falsely identified?** Check the comparison table for noise variables (true $\beta$ = 0) that were incorrectly selected by one or more methods. False positives are more likely for noise variables that are strongly correlated with true predictors.

**Which method is most conservative?** LASSO at lambda.1se tends to be the most conservative (fewest selections), because the "1 SE rule" deliberately chooses a sparser model. BMA with PIP $\geq$ 0.80 is moderately conservative. WALS with |t| $\geq$ 2 can be more liberal.

**When to use which method?**

| Method | Best for | Strengths | Limitations |
|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| BMA | Full uncertainty quantification | Probabilistic (PIPs), handles model uncertainty formally | Slower (MCMC), requires prior specification |
| LASSO | Prediction, sparse models | Fast, automatic selection, works with many variables | Binary (in/out), biased coefficients |
| WALS | Speed, frequentist inference | Very fast, produces t-statistics, no MCMC | Less common, limited software support |

The strongest recommendation: **use all three**. When they converge on the same variables, you have robust evidence. When they disagree, investigate why---the disagreement itself is informative.

```{r}
#| label: tbl-method-performance
#| code-summary: "Method performance summary table"

results_by_method |>
  mutate(
    sensitivity = true_pos / (true_pos + false_neg),  # true positive rate
    specificity = true_neg / (true_neg + false_pos),  # true negative rate
    accuracy    = (true_pos + true_neg) / 12          # overall accuracy
  ) |>
  gt() |>
  tab_header(
    title = "Method Performance Summary",
    subtitle = "Compared against the known ground truth (7 true, 5 noise)"
  ) |>
  cols_label(
    method      = "Method",
    true_pos    = "True Pos.",
    false_pos   = "False Pos.",
    false_neg   = "False Neg.",
    true_neg    = "True Neg.",
    sensitivity = "Sensitivity",
    specificity = "Specificity",
    accuracy    = "Accuracy"
  ) |>
  fmt_number(columns = c(sensitivity, specificity, accuracy), decimals = 3) |>
  tab_footnote("Sensitivity = true positive rate; Specificity = true negative rate.")
```

:::{.callout-note}
## What we learned
The three methods largely converge: strong true predictors are identified by all three, and noise variables are mostly excluded. The convergence across methods---Bayesian (BMA), penalized likelihood (LASSO), and frequentist model averaging (WALS)---provides strong evidence for robustness. Variables with very small true effects are harder for all methods to detect.
:::


# Conclusion {#sec-conclusion}

## Summary

This tutorial introduced three principled approaches to the variable selection problem:

1. **Bayesian Model Averaging (BMA)** averages over all possible models, weighting each by its posterior probability. It produces Posterior Inclusion Probabilities (PIPs) that quantify how robust each variable is across the entire model space. Variables with PIP $\geq$ 0.80 are considered robust.

2. **LASSO** adds an L1 penalty to the OLS objective, forcing irrelevant coefficients to exactly zero. Cross-validation selects the penalty strength. Post-LASSO recovers unbiased coefficient estimates for the selected variables.

3. **WALS** uses a semi-orthogonal transformation to decompose the model-averaging problem into independent subproblems---one per variable. It is extremely fast and produces familiar t-statistics for robustness assessment.

## Key Takeaways

**The methods converge.** When BMA, LASSO, and WALS agree that a variable matters (or does not matter), we can be confident in the conclusion. The convergence across fundamentally different statistical paradigms---Bayesian, penalized likelihood, and frequentist model averaging---provides a form of methodological triangulation.

**Model uncertainty is real but addressable.** With 12 candidate variables, there are 4,096 possible models. Rather than pretending one of them is "the" model, these methods account for the uncertainty explicitly. The result is more honest inference.

**Synthetic data lets us verify.** Because we designed the data-generating process, we could check each method's performance against the known truth. In practice, the truth is unknown---which is precisely why using multiple methods is so valuable.

## Applying This to Your Own Research

The code in this tutorial is designed to be modular. To apply these methods to your own data:

1. **Replace the CSV**: load your own cross-sectional dataset instead of the synthetic one
2. **Define the variable list**: specify which variables are candidates for selection
3. **Run the three methods**: use the same BMS, glmnet, and WALS function calls
4. **Compare results**: build the same comparison table and heatmap

The interpretation framework---PIPs for BMA, selection for LASSO, t-statistics for WALS---applies regardless of the specific dataset.

## Further Reading

- **BMA**: Hoeting, J.A., Madigan, D., Raftery, A.E., and Volinsky, C.T. (1999). "Bayesian Model Averaging: A Tutorial." *Statistical Science*, 14(4), 382--417.
- **LASSO**: Tibshirani, R. (1996). "Regression Shrinkage and Selection via the Lasso." *Journal of the Royal Statistical Society, Series B*, 58(1), 267--288.
- **WALS**: Magnus, J.R., Powell, O., and Prufer, P. (2010). "A Comparison of Two Model Averaging Techniques with an Application to Growth Empirics." *Journal of Econometrics*, 154(2), 139--153.
- **Application**: Aller, C., Ductor, L., and Grechyna, D. (2021). "Robust Determinants of CO~2~ Emissions." *Energy Economics*, 96, 105154.
- **R Packages**: [BMS vignette](https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/BMS/vignettes/bms.pdf), [glmnet vignette](https://glmnet.stanford.edu/articles/glmnet.html), [WALS package](https://cran.r-project.org/package=WALS)

:::{.callout-tip}
## The bottom line
No single method should be trusted in isolation. When BMA, LASSO, and WALS converge on the same set of robust determinants, you have strong evidence that these variables genuinely matter---regardless of which model specification you choose. That is the power of robust variable selection.
:::


# Session Info

```{r}
#| label: session-info
#| code-summary: "Session information for reproducibility"

sessionInfo()
```
